state_description: Waiting for human approval of the plan
product_description: In-vehicle infotainment system with Android Automotive integration, voice assistant, navigation, media streaming, and OTA update capability. ISO 26262 QM.

plan.description: Conduct competitive analysis of IVI market: Android Automotive OEM adopters (Volvo, GM, Renault), competing platforms (QNX BlackBerry, GENIVI), voice assistant landscape (Google Assistant, Alexa Auto, custom), and OTA providers (HARMAN, Aptiv). Define target OEM tier and feature-set positioning.

scope: Navigation SDK comparison (HERE, Google Maps Platform, Mapbox)

acceptance_criteria: Competitor matrix covers ≥8 IVI platforms with feature-by-feature comparison
Market sizing includes TAM/SAM/SOM with cited sources
Navigation SDK recommendation includes cost, offline capability, and Android Automotive compatibility matrix
OTA platform comparison covers SOTA and FOTA variants

plan.description: Produce Product Requirements Document (PRD) for the infotainment_system solution. Define ISO 26262 QM scope boundary, feature list (Android Automotive OS, voice assistant, navigation, media streaming, OTA), success metrics, and phased roadmap (MVP → GA → Post-launch).
acceptance_criteria: PRD covers all five feature areas with functional and non-functional requirements
ISO 26262 QM ASIL determination documented per feature with rationale
Success metrics are quantitative (e.g., voice command success rate ≥95%, OTA update success rate ≥99.5%)
Roadmap has defined MVP scope with locked acceptance criteria per milestone

plan.description: Conduct driver-focused UX research and produce wireframes for all IVI surfaces: Home screen, Navigation view, Media player, Voice assistant overlay, Settings, and OTA notification flow. Apply NHTSA distraction guidelines (≤2s glance time) and accessibility audit (WCAG 2.1 AA for in-vehicle context).

constraints: Driver distraction: max 2s single glance, 12s total task

acceptance_criteria: All primary tasks completable within NHTSA 12-second glance budget
Wireframes cover all six surfaces with light/dark mode variants
Accessibility audit identifies and resolves all AA-level contrast failures
Voice assistant overlay designed for eyes-free operation with audio-only fallback
App store screenshot mockups prepared
Onboarding flow under 3 steps

plan.description: Perform ISO 26262 QM Hazard Analysis and Risk Assessment (HARA) and system-level FMEA for all IVI features. Document item definition, hazardous event catalog, controllability classification, and ASIL determination. Produce Functional Safety Concept (FSC) for OTA update and navigation features as highest-risk surfaces.

payload.asil_target: QM (Quality Managed) — confirm no ASIL A/B/C/D items in scope
analysis_scope: OTA update (FOTA/SOTA): unintended vehicle behavior during update
Voice command misinterpretation in safety-critical context

acceptance_criteria: All hazardous events rated with Severity × Exposure × Controllability per ISO 26262 Table 4
ASIL determination confirmed QM for all in-scope items with documented rationale
Safety goals defined for OTA and navigation with verification method specified
FMEA covers ≥30 failure modes with detection and mitigation columns populated
ASIL level determined for each function
Fault tree analysis complete

plan.description: Establish compliance framework for AUTOSAR, ISO 26262 QM, and UNECE R155/R156. Create Design History File (DHF) skeleton, traceability matrix template (requirements → design → test), and V&V protocol. Map UNECE R155 cybersecurity management obligations and R156 software update management obligations to architecture decisions.
acceptance_criteria: DHF skeleton includes all ISO 26262 Part 4 work products with status tracking
RTM links every PRD requirement to at least one design artifact and one test case
V&V protocol defines pass/fail criteria for each test phase (unit, integration, HIL, system)
UNECE R155 gap analysis identifies ≥5 cybersecurity controls required and assigned owners
UNECE R156 SUMS checklist covers OTA authorization, rollback, and integrity verification

plan.description: Set up infotainment_system development environment: Android Automotive OS (AAOS) emulator and build system (AOSP with car extensions), AUTOSAR toolchain (Vector CANoe/DaVinci or open-source equivalent), cross-compilation toolchain (ARM Cortex-A / QNX or Linux), OTA test infrastructure (local update server), and CI/CD pipeline stubs. Configure agentic pattern: multi-agent coordinator routing voice, navigation, media, and OTA specialist agents.

toolchain.emulator: Android Automotive Emulator (AVD with car APIs)
toolchain.ota_server: Local OTA test server (A/B partition update)
toolchain.ci: GitHub Actions with AAOS emulator runner

acceptance_criteria: AAOS emulator boots and displays car launcher in <90 seconds
AOSP car build compiles without errors for x86_64 emulator target
Cross-compilation toolchain produces ARM ELF binary from sample C file
Local OTA server accepts and serves a test update package
CI pipeline runs build + unit tests on PR creation
Coordinator agent routes test task to correct specialist and returns merged result

plan.description: Implement Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL) modules for IVI hardware interfaces: display controller (DSI/LVDS), touch input (I2C capacitive), audio codec (I2S/TDM), CAN bus interface (vehicle data — speed, gear, ignition state), and GPS/GNSS receiver (UART/SPI). Implement RTOS task scheduler (FreeRTOS or Zephyr) with priority assignment per functional domain.

payload.target_soc: ARM Cortex-A53/A72 (e.g., NXP i.MX8 or Qualcomm SA8155P class)
payload.rtos: FreeRTOS (safety-qualified variant for QM) or Zephyr RTOS
hal_modules: display_hal.c — DSI/LVDS init, framebuffer, brightness control
touch_hal.c — I2C capacitive touch, gesture debounce
audio_hal.c — I2S codec init, volume, mute, source select
can_hal.c — CAN 2.0B frame receive, vehicle signal decoder (DBC-based)
gnss_hal.c — NMEA/UBX parser, fix acquisition, RTCM support

acceptance_criteria: All five HAL modules compile without warnings for ARM target
display_hal initializes at target resolution (1280×720 minimum) with verified pixel output
can_hal decodes vehicle speed signal from DBC file with ±1 km/h accuracy in test harness
gnss_hal achieves first fix within 60 seconds in open-sky simulation
RTOS task schedule verified with logic analyzer: no priority inversion under load
All HAL functions have Doxygen headers with parameter and return documentation
AUTOSAR compliance checked
MISRA C violations zero

plan.description: Build hardware simulation testbench for IVI SoC peripherals: SystemC/TLM model of CAN bus with vehicle ECU simulator, GNSS signal simulator (replay of recorded NMEA traces), and SPI/I2C bus model for touch and audio codec. Integrate with QEMU for full firmware bring-up without physical hardware.

sim_tools: CAN bus simulator (virtual CAN — vcan0 via SocketCAN)
models: can_ecu_sim.sc — SystemC ECU model sending speed/gear/ignition CAN frames
gnss_sim.py — NMEA sentence replay at 1Hz/10Hz for route simulation
i2c_touch_model.sc — touch event injection over simulated I2C
audio_loopback_model — I2S loopback for codec validation

acceptance_criteria: QEMU boots firmware image and reaches HAL init stage without hang
CAN ECU simulator generates valid DBC-encoded frames verified by can_hal_tb
GNSS simulator replays 100km route trace; gnss_hal_tb verifies coordinate accuracy within 10m
All testbenches runnable with single `make sim` command
Simulation coverage: ≥80% of HAL module branches exercised by testbench

plan.description: Define API contracts for all IVI service boundaries: Android Automotive Car API extensions (CarAudioManager, CarNavigationStatusManager, CarVoiceInteractionSession), OTA update REST API (manifest fetch, chunk download, status reporting), Voice NLU API (intent + entity schema), and Navigation SDK integration contract (route request/response, map tile protocol).

api_specs: ota_api.yaml — OpenAPI 3.1: GET /manifest, POST /download/chunk, GET /status/{job_id}, POST /apply, POST /rollback
voice_nlu_api.yaml — intents: navigate_to, play_media, call_contact, set_temperature, check_eta

plan.description: Implement OTA Update Server: manifest generation and signing (RSA-2048 or Ed25519), firmware/software package repository, chunked download with resume support, update campaign management (vehicle targeting, rollout percentage), and audit logging for UNECE R156 SUMS compliance. Stack: Python FastAPI + PostgreSQL + S3-compatible object storage.

features: Package upload + integrity hash (SHA-256) + signature verification
Campaign management: target by VIN, region, software version
Rollback trigger: automatic on error-rate threshold breach
UNECE R156 audit log: every update action logged with VIN, timestamp, actor
security: Package signing with HSM-backed private key

acceptance_criteria: OTA server boots and passes /health endpoint check
Package upload, signing, and SHA-256 verification pipeline completes in <30s for 500MB package

plan.description: Implement Voice Assistant Backend: wake-word detection integration (Picovoice Porcupine or Snowboy), speech-to-text pipeline (on-device via Android SpeechRecognizer or cloud via Whisper API), NLU intent classifier (fine-tuned on automotive domain), text-to-speech (Android TTS or Coqui TTS), and dialog state manager. Expose as Android Service bound to CarVoiceInteractionSession.

plan.description: Implement OTA client firmware module: A/B partition manager, update download agent (chunked HTTPS with TLS 1.3), package integrity verifier (SHA-256 + signature check against OTA server public key), atomic update applier (writes to inactive partition, flip on success), and watchdog-guarded rollback mechanism. Integrates with AUTOSAR NvM for persistent update state.

payload.standard: UNECE R156 SUMS — software update authorization, integrity, rollback
components: ota_client.c — state machine: IDLE → CHECK → DOWNLOAD → VERIFY → APPLY → CONFIRM
partition_mgr.c — A/B slot management, active/inactive flip, bootloader flag set
downloader.c — HTTPS chunked download, resume on disconnect, progress reporting
verifier.c — SHA-256 hash check, Ed25519 signature verification
rollback_wdt.c — hardware watchdog arm before apply; disarm after successful boot
safety_constraints: Update only when ignition OFF or vehicle parked
CAN signal gate: reject update if vehicle speed > 0 km/h
Battery level gate: reject if SOC < 20%

acceptance_criteria: OTA state machine traverses full lifecycle (IDLE→CONFIRM) without manual intervention in simulation
Signature verification rejects tampered package (bit-flip test) with logged SECURITY_EVENT
A/B partition flip verified: system boots from inactive partition post-apply
Watchdog rollback triggered and system returns to active (previous) partition on simulated boot failure
Speed gate tested: update download rejected when CAN speed signal > 0
UNECE R156 audit event emitted at each state transition with ISO 8601 timestamp
AUTOSAR compliance checked
MISRA C violations zero

plan.description: Perform threat modeling (STRIDE/TARA per ISO/SAE 21434) for the IVI system. Identify attack surfaces: OTA update channel, voice data pipeline, Bluetooth/WiFi interfaces, CAN bus gateway, and Android app sideloading. Produce UNECE R155 CSMS artifacts: cybersecurity goals, countermeasure catalog, and residual risk acceptance.

attack_surfaces: OTA channel: man-in-the-middle, package substitution, replay attack
Voice pipeline: adversarial audio commands, eavesdropping
BT/WiFi: pairing exploitation, rogue AP, BlueBorne-class
CAN gateway: message injection from IVI to powertrain (scope boundary)
App sideload: APK injection via USB, debug interface abuse
deliverables: CSMS evidence package for UNECE R155 type approval

acceptance_criteria: STRIDE analysis covers all six attack surfaces with threat rating (likelihood × impact)
Every high-rated threat has at least one countermeasure mapped and assigned to an implementation step
OTA channel: TLS 1.3 pinning and Ed25519 package signing confirmed as countermeasures
CAN gateway: IVI→CAN write access confirmed restricted to non-safety bus segments only
UNECE R155 CSMS artifact package complete: covers 7 mandatory cybersecurity measures

plan.description: Implement SAGE solution configuration for infotainment_system: prompts.yaml with specialist agent roles (IVI analyst, OTA operations agent, voice quality monitor, compliance tracker), tasks.yaml with 8 task types (ota_campaign_create, voice_quality_alert, navigation_error_triage, can_signal_anomaly, driver_feedback_analysis, software_update_audit, distraction_incident_review, compliance_gap_detect), and project.yaml with active modules and coordinator pattern. Agentic pattern: multi-agent coordinator routing IVI-specific events.

acceptance_criteria: project.yaml loads without validation errors via POST /config/switch {project: infotainment_system}
prompts.yaml defines ≥4 specialist agent roles with automotive-domain system prompts
tasks.yaml defines ≥8 task types with correct HITL levels (strict for OTA and compliance)
Coordinator routes test OTA failure event to ota_agent and returns structured proposal
HITL gate confirmed: ota_campaign_create creates pending proposal, does not auto-execute
Autonomous action confirmed: voice_quality_alert executes without approval gate
POST /analyze with IVI-domain payload returns structured analysis from correct specialist agent

flaws: Step 18 acceptance criteria states 'PR pipeline completes in <20 minutes (build + test + lint)' — AOSP full builds take 4–8 hours; incremental builds take 30–60 minutes minimum. This criterion is off by 10–20x and will be failed on day one.
Step 6 acceptance criteria states 'AAOS emulator boots in <90 seconds' — cold boot of the Android Automotive AVD routinely takes 3–8 minutes on standard CI hardware. This will never pass.

Step 4 pre-determines ASIL as QM before the HARA is performed. The HARA is the process that determines ASIL — you cannot set a target ASIL and then run the HARA to confirm it. Navigation misdirection and HMI freeze at highway speed may yield ASIL A under rigorous Severity × Exposure × Controllability analysis. This is methodologically backwards and will fail a third-party functional safety audit.

Step 15 (TARA/threat modeling) depends on steps 5 and 10, meaning the OTA server is fully implemented before the attack surface is analyzed. Security must inform design, not audit it post-hoc. UNECE R155 requires CSMS to be integrated into the development process, not appended to it.

Step 11 lists Snowboy as a wake-word detection option. Snowboy was archived in 2020 and is no longer maintained. Using deprecated security-adjacent software in a production vehicle system is a compliance and supply-chain risk.

Step 7 acceptance criteria demands 'MISRA C violations zero.' Zero MISRA violations is unachievable for any non-trivial embedded codebase. The correct standard is documented and justified deviations — claiming zero is either a lie or means the checker is not configured correctly.

Step 14 includes 'battery level gate: reject if SOC < 20%' — SOC applies to EVs and hybrids only. ICE vehicles use 12V lead-acid without meaningful SOC tracking. No equivalent 12V voltage gate is specified, leaving ICE platforms unprotected against low-voltage brick during OTA.

Step 10 requires HSM-backed OTA package signing, but Step 18 implements signing in GitHub Actions CI. There is no described mechanism for GitHub Actions to reach an HSM. This gap means signing will be done with software keys in practice, violating the stated security requirement.

Step 8 proposes using QEMU for AAOS firmware bring-up, but AAOS is not a standard QEMU target. AOSP car builds use the Android Automotive Emulator (AVD), not bare QEMU. Raw QEMU for NXP i.MX8 or SA8155P requires board-specific machine definitions that do not exist in upstream QEMU — this step will stall without custom QEMU porting work that is not scoped.

Step 12 specifies 'Jetpack Compose for Automotive (Car UI Library 2.0)' — as of 2025, Compose for Android Automotive remains experimental with known compatibility gaps against the AAOS Car UI Library. Production commitment to this stack requires explicit compatibility validation against the target AAOS version, which is absent.

Step 21 specifies an 8-hour soak test. Automotive reliability standards (e.g., AEC-Q100, OEM PPAP requirements) typically require 100–500 hours for thermal cycling and memory leak detection. An 8-hour soak will not catch slow leaks (e.g., a 5MB/hour leak looks fine at 8h, catastrophic at 200h).

The AUTOSAR Classic/Adaptive stack (step 5, step 7) and Android Automotive OS operate on physically separate compute domains (MCU vs application processor). The plan never defines the architectural boundary between them, inter-domain communication protocol (e.g., SOME/IP, virtio), or which stack owns which feature. Without this, steps 7 and 12 will produce incompatible artifacts.
suggestions: Move Step 15 (TARA) to immediately after Step 2 (PRD), before any architecture or implementation decisions. Security requirements must gate the API contracts in Step 9, not follow OTA server implementation.
Replace the AOSP build time acceptance criterion with a realistic split: 'incremental build <45 minutes; full clean build <6 hours on 32-core runner.' Use ccache and Google's build cache to optimize.

Add an explicit architectural document step (between Steps 6 and 7) that defines the MCU/AUTOSAR ↔ Android Automotive OS boundary, inter-domain IPC mechanism, and which compute domain owns each feature. This is the single most important missing artifact.

Replace Snowboy with a supported alternative: Picovoice Porcupine (actively maintained, has automotive licensing), SNIPS (acquired by Sonos but open-source artifacts exist), or custom wake-word model via TensorFlow Lite.
Replace 'MISRA C violations zero' with 'MISRA C:2012 compliance report generated; all Rule violations are documented deviations with rationale approved by safety_engineer.'

Add a secure boot chain step after Step 6: define hardware root of trust (ROM bootloader → U-Boot with verified boot → AAOS verified boot), key provisioning in manufacturing, and revocation strategy. Without this, UNECE R156 software integrity cannot be demonstrated.

Add certificate lifecycle management: PKI architecture, certificate expiry strategy for 10-year vehicle lifetime, OEM-specific root CA, and key rotation runbook. The current plan treats TLS/Ed25519 as implementation details with no operational lifecycle.

Add Android GMS licensing as a dependency blocker before Step 13. Google Maps for Android Automotive requires an OEM GMS contract and passing Android Automotive CDD compatibility testing. This is a 6–18 month business process, not a technical task.

Move HIL platform procurement decision to Step 6 (environment setup), not Step 19. dSPACE SCALEXIO lead times are 4–9 months. A decision deferred to Step 19 makes Steps 19–21 impossible to execute on schedule.

Add a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) step, required under GDPR Article 35 for voice recording pipelines and continuous location tracking — both present in this system. The current GDPR mention (step 16) is insufficient.
missing: Secure boot chain design and key provisioning in manufacturing — no mention anywhere in 24 steps. UNECE R156 Article 7 requires cryptographic integrity of installed software, which requires verified boot.

Vehicle network security gateway architecture — the CAN boundary is mentioned in security but no gateway design (e.g., Adaptive AUTOSAR ara::com, SOME/IP firewall, SecOC) is specified. This is the most critical safety boundary in the system.
Android GMS/GAPPS licensing process — required before any Google Maps for Automotive integration can proceed. Absent from the plan entirely.
PKI infrastructure and certificate lifecycle management across a 10+ year vehicle lifetime.

Multi-SoC porting strategy — NXP i.MX8 and Qualcomm SA8155P have fundamentally different BSPs, power management, and GPU stacks. The HAL layer must be specified as SoC-agnostic or the target must be locked to one SoC.
AUTOSAR ↔ AAOS inter-domain communication specification (SOME/IP, ARA::COM, or virtio-based).
Manufacturing key provisioning and hardware root of trust specification for production units.
Driver Monitoring System (DMS) integration point — EU GSR 2022 mandates DMS; IVI must receive DMS alerts and potentially suppress UI interactions.
DPIA (Data Protection Impact Assessment) for voice pipeline and location history — legally required under GDPR Article 35.
GPS/GNSS privacy — continuous location tracking requires user consent mechanism and data retention policy beyond the 30-day voice log policy already stated.

OSS license scanning in CI/CD — the SBOM GPL-3 check is a one-time compliance artifact (step 22) with no automated enforcement in the pipeline. GPL-3 components can be introduced silently via transitive dependencies.
Regulatory engagement plan with Technical Service (TS) and Type Approval Authority (TAA) for UNECE R155/R156 — type approval is not a document submission, it is an 8–24 month engagement process.
security_risks: TARA post-implementation: threat modeling after OTA server is built means discovered high-rated threats (e.g., unauthenticated manifest endpoint, weak VIN-based targeting) require expensive rework rather than cheap design changes.

HSM-CI gap: if GitHub Actions cannot reach the HSM, signing falls back to software keys on the runner. A compromised runner then signs malicious OTA packages that will be accepted by all enrolled vehicles. This is a fleet-wide remote code execution risk.

No secure boot: without verified boot, a physical attacker with USB access can reflash the application processor with arbitrary firmware. Combined with CAN gateway access, this is a potential vehicle safety issue even within the IVI QM scope.

Voice adversarial command surface: 'report_hazard' intent combined with automated responses could be triggered by audio played from a passing vehicle or roadside speaker. The plan's false-positive criterion (1/hour) only covers wake-word, not downstream intent misclassification.

CAN write access ambiguity: step 15 states 'IVI→CAN write access confirmed restricted to non-safety bus segments only' but no step in the plan implements or tests this restriction at the gateway level. It is a stated goal with no verification path.

OTA rollback integrity: the watchdog-guarded rollback in step 14 does not specify whether the rollback itself is authenticated. An attacker who can corrupt the active partition could force rollback to a known-vulnerable previous version.

mTLS vehicle identity: step 10 requires mTLS for vehicle-to-server communication but no step provisions vehicle certificates at manufacturing time or defines certificate rotation for the vehicle lifetime. Expired vehicle certs will brick OTA capability.

Debug interface: step 15 mentions APK injection via USB debug interface as an attack surface, and step 23 covers technician UDS access. Neither step specifies how ADB and diagnostic interfaces are disabled in production builds vs enabled for service.
final_review.summary: This plan is architecturally ambitious and structurally sound — 24 well-sequenced steps covering the full IVI stack from HARA through type approval. The dependency graph is mostly correct, the compliance artifacts are the right ones, and the HITL gating in step 24 is appropriately calibrated. However, it fails on several production-critical dimensions that would cause it to collapse before reaching the regulated-domain threshold of 85. The three most serious flaws are: (1) TARA placed after OTA server implementation, violating security-by-design and UNECE R155 process requirements; (2) AOSP build time acceptance criteria that are physically impossible, meaning CI will be declared broken from day one; and (3) the complete absence of a secure boot chain and PKI lifecycle — without these, UNECE R156 software integrity claims cannot be substantiated. Additionally, the HARA pre-determining QM before running the analysis is a methodological error that will surface in a third-party functional safety audit. The plan needs a security-architecture step inserted before Step 7, TARA moved to before Step 9, realistic build time targets, secure boot scoped in, and GMS licensing confirmed as a go/no-go gate before navigation implementation begins. In current form, this ships a technically impressive demo that fails regulatory submission.

flaws: Step 18 acceptance criteria states 'PR pipeline completes in <20 minutes (build + test + lint)' — AOSP full builds take 4–8 hours; incremental builds take 30–60 minutes minimum. This criterion is off by 10–20x and will be failed on day one.
Step 6 acceptance criteria states 'AAOS emulator boots in <90 seconds' — cold boot of the Android Automotive AVD routinely takes 3–8 minutes on standard CI hardware. This will never pass.

Step 4 pre-determines ASIL as QM before the HARA is performed. The HARA is the process that determines ASIL — you cannot set a target ASIL and then run the HARA to confirm it. Navigation misdirection and HMI freeze at highway speed may yield ASIL A under rigorous Severity × Exposure × Controllability analysis. This is methodologically backwards and will fail a third-party functional safety audit.

Step 15 (TARA/threat modeling) depends on steps 5 and 10, meaning the OTA server is fully implemented before the attack surface is analyzed. Security must inform design, not audit it post-hoc. UNECE R155 requires CSMS to be integrated into the development process, not appended to it.

Step 11 lists Snowboy as a wake-word detection option. Snowboy was archived in 2020 and is no longer maintained. Using deprecated security-adjacent software in a production vehicle system is a compliance and supply-chain risk.

Step 7 acceptance criteria demands 'MISRA C violations zero.' Zero MISRA violations is unachievable for any non-trivial embedded codebase. The correct standard is documented and justified deviations — claiming zero is either a lie or means the checker is not configured correctly.

Step 14 includes 'battery level gate: reject if SOC < 20%' — SOC applies to EVs and hybrids only. ICE vehicles use 12V lead-acid without meaningful SOC tracking. No equivalent 12V voltage gate is specified, leaving ICE platforms unprotected against low-voltage brick during OTA.

Step 10 requires HSM-backed OTA package signing, but Step 18 implements signing in GitHub Actions CI. There is no described mechanism for GitHub Actions to reach an HSM. This gap means signing will be done with software keys in practice, violating the stated security requirement.

Step 8 proposes using QEMU for AAOS firmware bring-up, but AAOS is not a standard QEMU target. AOSP car builds use the Android Automotive Emulator (AVD), not bare QEMU. Raw QEMU for NXP i.MX8 or SA8155P requires board-specific machine definitions that do not exist in upstream QEMU — this step will stall without custom QEMU porting work that is not scoped.

Step 12 specifies 'Jetpack Compose for Automotive (Car UI Library 2.0)' — as of 2025, Compose for Android Automotive remains experimental with known compatibility gaps against the AAOS Car UI Library. Production commitment to this stack requires explicit compatibility validation against the target AAOS version, which is absent.

Step 21 specifies an 8-hour soak test. Automotive reliability standards (e.g., AEC-Q100, OEM PPAP requirements) typically require 100–500 hours for thermal cycling and memory leak detection. An 8-hour soak will not catch slow leaks (e.g., a 5MB/hour leak looks fine at 8h, catastrophic at 200h).

The AUTOSAR Classic/Adaptive stack (step 5, step 7) and Android Automotive OS operate on physically separate compute domains (MCU vs application processor). The plan never defines the architectural boundary between them, inter-domain communication protocol (e.g., SOME/IP, virtio), or which stack owns which feature. Without this, steps 7 and 12 will produce incompatible artifacts.
suggestions: Move Step 15 (TARA) to immediately after Step 2 (PRD), before any architecture or implementation decisions. Security requirements must gate the API contracts in Step 9, not follow OTA server implementation.
Replace the AOSP build time acceptance criterion with a realistic split: 'incremental build <45 minutes; full clean build <6 hours on 32-core runner.' Use ccache and Google's build cache to optimize.

Add an explicit architectural document step (between Steps 6 and 7) that defines the MCU/AUTOSAR ↔ Android Automotive OS boundary, inter-domain IPC mechanism, and which compute domain owns each feature. This is the single most important missing artifact.

Replace Snowboy with a supported alternative: Picovoice Porcupine (actively maintained, has automotive licensing), SNIPS (acquired by Sonos but open-source artifacts exist), or custom wake-word model via TensorFlow Lite.
Replace 'MISRA C violations zero' with 'MISRA C:2012 compliance report generated; all Rule violations are documented deviations with rationale approved by safety_engineer.'

Add a secure boot chain step after Step 6: define hardware root of trust (ROM bootloader → U-Boot with verified boot → AAOS verified boot), key provisioning in manufacturing, and revocation strategy. Without this, UNECE R156 software integrity cannot be demonstrated.

Add certificate lifecycle management: PKI architecture, certificate expiry strategy for 10-year vehicle lifetime, OEM-specific root CA, and key rotation runbook. The current plan treats TLS/Ed25519 as implementation details with no operational lifecycle.

Add Android GMS licensing as a dependency blocker before Step 13. Google Maps for Android Automotive requires an OEM GMS contract and passing Android Automotive CDD compatibility testing. This is a 6–18 month business process, not a technical task.

Move HIL platform procurement decision to Step 6 (environment setup), not Step 19. dSPACE SCALEXIO lead times are 4–9 months. A decision deferred to Step 19 makes Steps 19–21 impossible to execute on schedule.

Add a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) step, required under GDPR Article 35 for voice recording pipelines and continuous location tracking — both present in this system. The current GDPR mention (step 16) is insufficient.

Vehicle network security gateway architecture — the CAN boundary is mentioned in security but no gateway design (e.g., Adaptive AUTOSAR ara::com, SOME/IP firewall, SecOC) is specified. This is the most critical safety boundary in the system.
Android GMS/GAPPS licensing process — required before any Google Maps for Automotive integration can proceed. Absent from the plan entirely.

Multi-SoC porting strategy — NXP i.MX8 and Qualcomm SA8155P have fundamentally different BSPs, power management, and GPU stacks. The HAL layer must be specified as SoC-agnostic or the target must be locked to one SoC.

history.summary: This plan is architecturally ambitious and structurally sound — 24 well-sequenced steps covering the full IVI stack from HARA through type approval. The dependency graph is mostly correct, the compliance artifacts are the right ones, and the HITL gating in step 24 is appropriately calibrated. However, it fails on several production-critical dimensions that would cause it to collapse before reaching the regulated-domain threshold of 85. The three most serious flaws are: (1) TARA placed after OTA server implementation, violating security-by-design and UNECE R155 process requirements; (2) AOSP build time acceptance criteria that are physically impossible, meaning CI will be declared broken from day one; and (3) the complete absence of a secure boot chain and PKI lifecycle — without these, UNECE R156 software integrity claims cannot be substantiated. Additionally, the HARA pre-determining QM before running the analysis is a methodological error that will surface in a third-party functional safety audit. The plan needs a security-architecture step inserted before Step 7, TARA moved to before Step 9, realistic build time targets, secure boot scoped in, and GMS licensing confirmed as a go/no-go gate before navigation implementation begins. In current form, this ships a technically impressive demo that fails regulatory submission.
